questions.question: Explain the difference between routing and forwarding in network layer.
keyPoints: Routing is the control plane function that determines optimal paths
Forwarding is the data plane function that moves packets from input to output
Routing is performed at network layer by routers
Forwarding involves lookup tables and header processing

questions.question: Describe the DHCP DORA process for IP address allocation.
keyPoints: DORA stands for Discover, Offer, Request, Acknowledgment
Client broadcasts DHCP Discover to find servers
Servers respond with DHCP Offer containing available IP
Client sends DHCP Request selecting an offer
Server sends DHCP Acknowledgment confirming allocation

questions.question: What is the purpose of TTL in IP packets? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: TTL prevents infinite packet loops in network
Each router decrements TTL by 1 before forwarding
When TTL reaches 0, packet is discarded
ICMP Time Exceeded message is sent to source
Example: TTL=64 means packet can traverse 64 routers max

questions.question: Differentiate between Distance Vector and Link State routing protocols.
keyPoints: Distance Vector exchanges entire routing tables with neighbors
Link State exchanges only link costs with all routers
Distance Vector uses hop count metric; Link State uses bandwidth/cost
Examples: RIP is Distance Vector, OSPF is Link State
Link State converges faster but requires more memory

questions.question: Explain Network Address Translation (NAT) and its need.
keyPoints: NAT maps private IP addresses to public IP addresses
Solves IPv4 address exhaustion problem
Allows multiple devices to share single public IP
Types: Static NAT, Dynamic NAT, PAT (Port Address Translation)
Provides basic security by hiding internal network addresses

questions.question: What is fragmentation in network layer? When does it occur?
keyPoints: Fragmentation splits large packets into smaller chunks
Occurs when packet size exceeds MTU of any network segment
Each fragment gets its own IP header with identification preserved
More Fragments flag set on all but last fragment
Offset field indicates fragment position in original packet

questions.question: Explain the IPv4 header fields: Version, IHL, TTL, Protocol, and Source/Destination Address.
keyPoints: Version: IP version number (4 for IPv4)
IHL: Internet Header Length in 32-bit words (min 5)
TTL: Time To Live, max hop count
Protocol: Upper layer protocol (6=TCP, 17=UDP, 1=ICMP)
Source/Destination Address: 32-bit IP addresses of sender and receiver

questions.question: Explain the IPv4 packet header structure in detail. Draw and label all fields.
keyPoints: Draw IPv4 header showing all fields with sizes
Version (4 bits): IP version
IHL (4 bits): Header length in 32-bit words
Type of Service (8 bits): QoS parameters
Total Length (16 bits): Total packet size
Identification (16 bits): Packet ID for fragmentation
Flags (3 bits): Don't Fragment, More Fragments
Fragment Offset (13 bits): Position in original packet
TTL (8 bits): Hop limit
Protocol (8 bits): Upper layer protocol
Header Checksum (16 bits): Error detection
Source Address (32 bits): Sender IP
Destination Address (32 bits): Receiver IP
Options (variable): Optional fields

questions.question: Describe Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm used in link state routing. Explain with example network.
keyPoints: Algorithm initializes source node with distance 0, others infinity
At each step, select unvisited node with minimum distance
Update distances to neighbors considering path through selected node
Continue until all nodes visited
Draw example network with costs
Show step-by-step calculation of shortest path table
Time complexity: O(V²) or O(E log V) with heap

questions.question: Explain the concept of subnetting. Given IP address 192.168.10.0/24, create 4 subnets of equal size and show subnet masks, network addresses, and usable host ranges.
keyPoints: Subnetting borrows bits from host portion for subnet field
To create 4 subnets, need 2 bits (2²=4)
New prefix length: /26 (24+2)
Usable hosts per subnet: 62 (64-2 for network and broadcast)

questions.question: Explain the Bellman-Ford distance vector routing algorithm with an example. Also mention count-to-infinity problem.
keyPoints: Bellman-Ford equation: D(x,y) = min{c(x,v) + D(v,y)}
Each router maintains distance vector (destination, cost, next hop)
Periodically exchanges vectors with neighbors
Example: Show table updates for 3-router network
Count-to-infinity: Problem where routes slowly count to infinity when link breaks
Solution: Split horizon, Poison reverse
Maximum hop count (16) terminates counting
